title: Why We Built KeepUp
excerpt: The story behind KeepUp: why a team of ISP engineers decided to build a better uptime monitoring platform, and what makes it different from UptimeRobot, Pingdom, and the rest.
content:
It was 3:17 AM on a Tuesday when the alert came in. A core router at one of our client ISPs had gone down, taking 2,000 subscribers offline in a mid-sized city in southern Brazil. The NOC team scrambled. But the real problem was not the router failure itself — it was that the team found out 14 minutes after customers started calling, not from their monitoring tools.

That night was a turning point for us. We had been running ISP operations for years, and we knew the monitoring tools on the market were not built for the way we worked. That frustration eventually became KeepUp.

The Problem with Existing Tools

We tried everything. UptimeRobot was a good start — affordable, simple, does the basics. But when you are managing infrastructure for an Internet Service Provider, the basics are not enough. We needed integration with IXC (the billing and provisioning system used by most Brazilian ISPs). We needed Zabbix data pulling into the same dashboard. We needed status pages that our non-technical support teams could understand and that our end customers could trust.

Pingdom was powerful but expensive. For a Brazilian ISP operating on tight margins with BRL-denominated revenue and USD-denominated SaaS costs, the math simply did not work. StatusPage.io from Atlassian solved the status page problem beautifully, but it was only a status page — you still needed a separate tool for monitoring, and another for alerting, and somehow you had to glue them all together.

We kept coming back to the same conclusion: no single tool combined monitoring, alerting, and status pages in a way that actually worked for our use case.

Born from ISP Operations

KeepUp was born inside IuriLabs, a small technology company in Brazil that had been building software for ISPs since 2020. We understood the Brazilian telecom market intimately. We knew that ISPs in Brazil need:

  • IXC Integration — to monitor services and equipment directly from the provisioning system, without manual configuration for every subscriber CPE.
  • Zabbix Integration — because most ISPs already run Zabbix for network monitoring, and duplicating that data in a separate tool is wasteful.
  • Portuguese Language Support — not just translated strings, but genuinely localized UI, notifications, and status pages that make sense in PT-BR.
  • LGPD Compliance — Brazil’s data protection law is real, and monitoring tools that store customer data need to respect it.
  • Affordable Pricing in BRL — no more converting USD prices and wincing at the exchange rate.

These were not nice-to-haves. These were requirements. And no tool on the market checked all the boxes.

What Makes KeepUp Different

We set out to build the tool we wished existed. Here is what we focused on:

Monitoring + Status Pages in One Platform

With KeepUp, you do not need three separate subscriptions. Your monitors feed directly into your status pages. When a service goes down, the status page updates automatically. When it comes back up, so does the status page. No Zapier glue. No webhook middleware. It just works.

Nine Alert Channels, Not Just Email

Email is fine for some alerts. But at 3 AM, you need something that will actually wake you up. KeepUp supports Email, Slack, Discord, Telegram, PagerDuty, OpsGenie, Webhooks, SMS, and Microsoft Teams. You can set up notification policies with escalation chains and cooldown periods, so you are not drowning in duplicate alerts during a major incident.

Built for Multi-Tenancy

We built KeepUp as a SaaS platform from the ground up, with proper multi-tenancy, organization isolation, and role-based access control. Whether you are a solo developer monitoring your side project or an MSP managing infrastructure for dozens of clients, the platform scales with you.

ISP-Native Features

For ISPs, KeepUp offers what no other monitoring SaaS does: native IXC and Zabbix integrations. Pull PPPoE session data, monitor ONU equipment status, track bandwidth utilization — all from the same dashboard where you monitor your web applications and APIs. This is not a generic webhook integration. These are purpose-built adapters that speak the same protocols your network equipment does.

Beautiful, Branded Status Pages

Your customers deserve better than a plain white page with green checkmarks. KeepUp status pages support custom domains, custom branding, multiple languages, subscriber notifications, password protection, and maintenance windows. They are designed to build trust during incidents, not just display data.

The Brazilian Market Opportunity

Brazil has over 20,000 registered ISPs. Most are small to medium-sized operations serving anywhere from 500 to 50,000 subscribers. These companies are the backbone of internet access in Brazil — especially outside the major metropolitan areas where the big telcos operate.

These ISPs are increasingly sophisticated. They run FTTH networks, manage complex routing topologies, and serve customers who expect the same uptime as any major cloud provider. But their tooling has not kept up. Many still rely on a patchwork of Zabbix, Grafana, WhatsApp groups, and manual processes to manage incidents.

KeepUp was designed specifically for this market. We understand the workflows, the integrations, the language, and the economics. We are not a Silicon Valley SaaS trying to add ISP features as an afterthought. We are ISP people who built a monitoring platform.

What is Next

We launched KeepUp in April 2026, and we are just getting started. Our roadmap includes:

  • Advanced Analytics — SLA reporting, uptime trends, response time percentiles.
  • Incident Postmortems — collaborative incident timelines with root cause analysis templates.
  • Mobile App — native iOS and Android apps for on-call engineers.
  • More Integrations — Datadog, New Relic, AWS CloudWatch, and more.
  • AI-Powered Insights — pattern detection, anomaly alerts, and predictive downtime warnings.

But more than features, we are building a community. We want to hear from every SRE, DevOps engineer, NOC operator, and ISP technician who has felt the pain of inadequate monitoring tools. Your feedback shapes our roadmap.

Try KeepUp

If any of this resonates with you, give KeepUp a try. The free plan includes 5 monitors, email alerts, and a status page — enough to see if the platform fits your workflow. No credit card required. No 14-day trial that expires before you have time to evaluate it properly.

We built KeepUp because we needed it. We think you might need it too.

— The KeepUp Team at IuriLabs

title: Top 10 Uptime Monitoring Tools in 2026: The Definitive Guide
excerpt: We tested and compared the 10 best uptime monitoring tools of 2026, including KeepUp, UptimeRobot, Pingdom, BetterUptime, and more. See our honest rankings with pricing, pros, and cons.
content:
Choosing the right uptime monitoring tool in 2026 is harder than ever. The market has matured, prices have shifted, and new players have emerged with genuinely innovative approaches. Whether you are running a SaaS platform, an e-commerce store, or an Internet Service Provider, you need a tool that goes beyond simple ping checks.

We spent three months testing and evaluating the most popular uptime monitoring tools on the market. We ran identical monitors across all platforms, compared alert delivery times, evaluated status page features, and calculated the real cost at various scales. Here is what we found.

Our Evaluation Criteria

Before diving into the rankings, here is what we measured:

  • Check types and flexibility — HTTP, ping, port, DNS, keyword, API, and custom checks
  • Alert channels and speed — how many notification channels and how fast alerts arrive
  • Status pages — built-in, customizable, subscriber notifications
  • Integrations — third-party tools, webhooks, and infrastructure connectors
  • Pricing fairness — value per monitor at different scales
  • Ease of use — setup time, UI quality, documentation

The Top 10 Uptime Monitoring Tools in 2026

Rank Tool Best For Starting Price Free Plan Status Pages
1KeepUpISPs, SaaS, all-in-one$9/moYes (5 monitors)Built-in
2BetterUptimeSaaS and DevOps teams$20/moYes (limited)Built-in
3UptimeRobotBudget-conscious teams$7/moYes (50 monitors)Built-in
4PingdomEnterprise with budget$15/moNoAdd-on
5DatadogFull-stack observability$15/host/moLimitedNo
6InstatusBeautiful status pages$20/moYes (limited)Built-in
7StatusPage.ioAtlassian ecosystem$29/moNoBuilt-in
8FreshpingFreshworks usersFreeYes (50 checks)Built-in
9Hetrix ToolsServer and blacklist monitoring$10/moYes (15 monitors)Built-in
10CachetSelf-hosted open sourceFree (self-hosted)N/ABuilt-in

1. KeepUp — Best All-in-One Monitoring Platform

KeepUp combines uptime monitoring, alerting, and status pages in a single platform. What sets it apart is native support for ISP infrastructure: IXC integration, Zabbix data imports, and PPPoE monitoring are built in, not bolted on. The nine alert channels with escalation policies mean the right person gets notified at the right time. The free tier includes 5 monitors and a status page, making it easy to evaluate.

Pros: All-in-one platform, ISP-native features, 9 alert channels, affordable pricing, Portuguese language support, LGPD compliant.

Cons: Newer to market than some competitors, fewer third-party integrations than Datadog.

2. BetterUptime — Best for SaaS Teams

BetterUptime has built a polished product with excellent incident management workflows. The on-call scheduling and escalation features are particularly strong. Their status pages are clean and professional.

Pros: Excellent UX, strong on-call scheduling, good integrations, solid status pages.

Cons: More expensive at scale, no ISP-specific features, limited customization on lower tiers.

3. UptimeRobot — Best Budget Option

UptimeRobot remains the most generous free tier in the market with 50 monitors at 5-minute intervals. For small teams who need basic uptime monitoring without the bells and whistles, it is hard to beat.

Pros: Generous free tier, simple interface, reliable basic monitoring, affordable paid plans.

Cons: Limited alert channels on free plan, basic status pages, no escalation policies, 5-minute minimum interval on free tier.

4. Pingdom — Best for Enterprise

Now owned by SolarWinds, Pingdom offers robust synthetic monitoring and real user monitoring (RUM). It is a mature product with deep analytics capabilities.

Pros: RUM capabilities, transaction monitoring, detailed analytics, enterprise-grade reliability.

Cons: Expensive, no free plan, clunky UI that has not aged well, SolarWinds ownership concerns post-breach.

5. Datadog — Best for Full-Stack Observability

Datadog is not primarily an uptime monitoring tool, but its synthetic monitoring module is powerful. If you already use Datadog for APM and logging, adding uptime monitoring keeps everything in one place.

Pros: Incredible depth, APM integration, custom dashboards, massive ecosystem.

Cons: Expensive and complex pricing, overkill for simple uptime monitoring, steep learning curve, no built-in status pages.

6. Instatus — Best Status Page Design

Instatus focuses primarily on beautiful status pages with some monitoring capabilities. If your main goal is a gorgeous, branded status page, Instatus delivers.

Pros: Beautiful status pages, modern design, good branding options, reasonable pricing.

Cons: Limited monitoring features, fewer alert channels, not ideal as a standalone monitoring tool.

7. StatusPage.io (Atlassian) — Best for Atlassian Users

StatusPage.io is the industry standard for status pages, but it is only a status page. You need a separate monitoring tool to feed data into it. If you are already in the Atlassian ecosystem with Jira and Opsgenie, the integration is seamless.

Pros: Industry-standard status pages, excellent Atlassian integration, trusted brand.

Cons: Not a monitoring tool, expensive for what it does, requires separate monitoring subscription.

8. Freshping — Best Free Tier for Basics

Freshping, part of the Freshworks suite, offers a solid free tier with 50 checks and 1-minute intervals. It integrates well with other Freshworks products like Freshdesk and Freshservice.

Pros: Generous free tier, 1-minute checks for free, Freshworks ecosystem integration.

Cons: Limited features beyond basics, tied to Freshworks ecosystem, minimal customization.

9. Hetrix Tools — Best for Server Monitoring

Hetrix Tools combines uptime monitoring with server resource monitoring and blacklist monitoring. It is a solid choice for teams that need to monitor server health alongside uptime.

Pros: Server resource monitoring, blacklist monitoring, affordable, good free tier.

Cons: Less polished UI, smaller community, fewer integrations than top-tier tools.

10. Cachet — Best Self-Hosted Open Source

Cachet is an open-source status page system you host yourself. It gives you complete control over your data and infrastructure. However, development has slowed significantly, and it lacks built-in monitoring.

Pros: Open source, self-hosted, full data control, free.

Cons: No built-in monitoring, slow development, requires server maintenance, PHP-based (older codebase).

Which Tool Should You Choose?

The answer depends on your needs:

  • ISPs and telecom providers: KeepUp is the clear choice with IXC, Zabbix, and PPPoE monitoring built in.
  • SaaS companies: KeepUp or BetterUptime, depending on whether you need ISP features or on-call scheduling.
  • Budget-conscious teams: UptimeRobot for monitoring basics, KeepUp for the all-in-one free tier.
  • Enterprise with deep pockets: Datadog for full-stack observability, Pingdom for synthetic monitoring.
  • Status page only: Instatus for design, StatusPage.io for enterprise credibility.
  • Self-hosted requirement: Cachet, though be prepared for maintenance overhead.

No matter which tool you choose, the important thing is that you have uptime monitoring in place. Your customers expect it, your SLAs demand it, and your team deserves tools that help rather than hinder incident response.

Ready to try the all-in-one approach? Start free with KeepUp — 5 monitors, 9 alert channels, and a status page included. No credit card required.

title: How to Create a Status Page for Your ISP (Step-by-Step Guide)
excerpt: A complete step-by-step guide to creating a professional status page for your Internet Service Provider. Cover legal requirements, customer trust, and the tools that make it easy.
content:
If you run an Internet Service Provider, you already know what happens when there is an outage: your phone lines light up, your support inbox overflows, and your team spends more time answering "Is the internet down?" than actually fixing the problem. A well-built status page changes this dynamic entirely.

This guide walks you through the entire process of creating a status page for your ISP, from planning to launch. Whether you serve 500 subscribers or 50,000, the principles are the same.

Why ISPs Need Status Pages

Status pages are not just a nice-to-have for ISPs. They are a critical piece of your customer communication infrastructure. Here is why:

1. Reduce Support Ticket Volume

During a major outage, an ISP with 10,000 subscribers can receive hundreds of support calls in the first 30 minutes. Each call costs your team time and your company money. A public status page can reduce inbound support tickets by 30-50% during incidents because customers can check the status page instead of calling.

2. Legal and Regulatory Requirements

In many jurisdictions, ISPs have transparency obligations. In Brazil, ANATEL requires ISPs to communicate service disruptions to subscribers. A status page provides a documented, timestamped record of your communication during incidents, which can be crucial during regulatory audits.

3. Build Customer Trust and Loyalty

Customers do not leave ISPs because of outages. They leave because of poor communication during outages. A status page that is honest, timely, and informative tells your customers that you take their service seriously. That builds the kind of trust that survives the occasional network hiccup.

4. Improve Internal Communication

A status page is not just for customers. It also serves as a single source of truth for your internal teams. When support, NOC, and management are all looking at the same status page, everyone is on the same page about what is happening, what is affected, and what is being done.

Step-by-Step: Creating Your ISP Status Page

Step 1: Define Your Service Components

Start by listing every service your customers interact with. For a typical ISP, this might include:

  • Internet Access — your core product, broken down by region or technology (FTTH, cable, wireless)
  • DNS Servers — if you run your own recursive resolvers
  • Email Service — if you provide email hosting to subscribers
  • Customer Portal — the web interface where subscribers manage their accounts
  • VoIP Service — if you offer voice services
  • TV/Streaming — if you offer IPTV or streaming bundles
  • Billing System — payment processing and invoicing

Be specific but not overwhelming. Group services logically. Your customers do not need to know about every internal microservice, but they do need to see the services they use every day.

Step 2: Set Up Monitoring for Each Component

Your status page is only as good as the monitoring behind it. Each service component needs at least one monitor that checks its availability. For ISPs, this typically means:

  • Ping monitors for core network equipment (routers, switches, OLTs)
  • HTTP monitors for web-based services (customer portal, billing system)
  • Port monitors for specific services (DNS on port 53, SMTP on port 25)
  • IXC integration for provisioning and billing system health
  • Zabbix integration for pulling existing network monitoring data

KeepUp supports all of these monitor types and automatically updates your status page when a monitor detects an issue.

Step 3: Choose Your Status Page Platform

You have three options:

  1. Build it yourself — maximum control, maximum maintenance burden. Only recommended if you have dedicated development resources.
  2. Use a standalone status page tool — tools like StatusPage.io or Instatus give you a status page, but you need separate monitoring. This means gluing two tools together with webhooks.
  3. Use an all-in-one platform — tools like KeepUp combine monitoring and status pages, so your monitors automatically feed your status page. No glue code, no webhook maintenance.

For ISPs, we strongly recommend the all-in-one approach. You have enough infrastructure to manage already. Your monitoring tool and your status page should be the same thing.

Step 4: Configure Your Status Page

Once you have chosen your platform, configure the following:

  • Custom domain — use status.yourisp.com or similar. Your customers should recognize the domain.
  • Branding — match your ISP's colors, logo, and visual identity. A branded status page looks professional and trustworthy.
  • Language — if you serve customers in multiple languages, your status page should too. KeepUp supports multiple languages out of the box.
  • Subscriber notifications — let customers subscribe to updates via email or SMS. This is powerful because it means customers get notified proactively instead of discovering outages on their own.

Step 5: Create Incident Templates

During an outage, your team is under pressure. Having pre-written incident templates saves time and ensures consistent communication. Create templates for common scenarios:

  • Network outage — "We are investigating reports of connectivity issues in [region]. Our team is actively working to identify and resolve the cause."
  • Scheduled maintenance — "We will be performing scheduled maintenance on [service] from [start] to [end]. Some subscribers may experience brief interruptions."
  • Degraded performance — "We are aware of slower-than-usual speeds in [region]. Our network team is investigating."
  • Resolved — "The issue affecting [service] has been resolved. All systems are operating normally. Thank you for your patience."

Step 6: Train Your Team

A status page is only useful if your team actually updates it. Train your NOC operators, support team leads, and on-call engineers on:

  • When to create an incident (answer: as soon as you know about it)
  • How to update the status page (should take less than 30 seconds)
  • What language to use (clear, non-technical, empathetic)
  • When to resolve an incident (only after confirming the fix is stable)

Step 7: Announce Your Status Page

Once your status page is live, tell your customers about it. Add a link in your customer portal, include it in your email signatures, mention it in your next newsletter, and train your support team to direct callers to the status page during incidents.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

After helping dozens of ISPs set up status pages, we have seen these mistakes repeatedly:

  1. Too many components — showing 50 internal services confuses customers. Keep it to the services they actually use.
  2. Delayed updates — a status page that shows "all systems operational" during an outage is worse than no status page at all.
  3. Technical jargon — "BGP session flap on PE-router-03" means nothing to your subscribers. Write for humans.
  4. No subscriber notifications — if customers have to manually check the page, many will not. Enable email and SMS subscriptions.
  5. Forgetting maintenance windows — always post scheduled maintenance in advance. Surprises erode trust.

Get Started Today

Creating a status page for your ISP does not have to be complicated. With KeepUp, you can have monitors running and a branded status page live in under 15 minutes. The free plan includes everything you need to start: 5 monitors, a customizable status page, and email alerts.

Your customers deserve to know what is happening with their internet service. Give them a status page that shows you care.

title: KeepUp vs UptimeRobot: A Detailed 2026 Comparison
excerpt: A head-to-head comparison of KeepUp and UptimeRobot in 2026. We break down features, pricing, alerting, status pages, and integrations to help you pick the right monitoring tool.
content:
UptimeRobot has been a go-to uptime monitoring tool for over a decade, and for good reason: it offers a generous free tier, a straightforward interface, and reliable basic monitoring. But as monitoring needs have evolved, many teams are looking for alternatives that offer more flexibility, better alerting, and integrated status pages. KeepUp is one of those alternatives, and in this article we will compare them head to head.

This is not a hit piece on UptimeRobot. It is a genuinely useful tool, and for some use cases it remains the best choice. Our goal is to help you understand the differences so you can make an informed decision.

Feature-by-Feature Comparison

Feature KeepUp UptimeRobot
Free monitors550
Free check interval3 minutes5 minutes
Paid check interval30 seconds1 minute
Monitor typesHTTP, Ping, Port, DNS, API, IXC, ZabbixHTTP, Ping, Port, Keyword, Heartbeat
Alert channels9 (Email, Slack, Discord, Telegram, PagerDuty, OpsGenie, Webhooks, SMS, Teams)12+ (Email, SMS, Slack, Telegram, Webhooks, and more)
Escalation policiesYes (step chains with cooldowns)No
Status pagesBranded, multi-language, subscriber notificationsBasic, limited customization on free plan
Custom domain status pagesYes (all plans)Yes (paid plans)
Multi-language status pagesYesNo
ISP integrations (IXC, Zabbix)YesNo
Maintenance windowsYesYes
Incident managementBuilt-in with acknowledgementBasic
Multi-tenancy (organizations)YesYes (Team plans)
API accessYes (REST)Yes (REST)
LGPD/GDPR complianceYesGDPR only

Where UptimeRobot Wins

Free Tier Generosity

UptimeRobot offers 50 free monitors to KeepUp's 5. If you need to monitor dozens of endpoints and your budget is literally zero, UptimeRobot's free plan is hard to beat. This is their strongest advantage, and we will not pretend otherwise.

Mature Ecosystem

UptimeRobot has been around since 2010. It has more third-party integrations, more community scripts, and more documentation than any newer tool. If you search for "how to monitor X with UptimeRobot," you will almost certainly find an answer.

Alert Channel Count

UptimeRobot supports more alert channels overall, including some niche options. If you need a specific alert integration that KeepUp does not yet support, check UptimeRobot's list first.

Where KeepUp Wins

Escalation Policies

This is a significant differentiator. UptimeRobot sends alerts, but it does not have true escalation policies. If the primary on-call engineer does not respond, UptimeRobot does not automatically escalate to the next person. KeepUp's notification policies support multi-step escalation chains with configurable delays and cooldown periods. For teams that take on-call seriously, this matters.

ISP-Specific Features

KeepUp was built for Internet Service Providers. It has native IXC and Zabbix integrations that let you monitor ISP infrastructure directly. UptimeRobot is a general-purpose tool with no ISP-specific features. If you run an ISP, this alone might be the deciding factor.

Status Page Quality

KeepUp's status pages are fully branded, support multiple languages, include subscriber notifications with email and SMS, and work on custom domains across all plans. UptimeRobot's status pages are functional but more limited in customization, especially on the free plan.

Incident Management

KeepUp includes incident acknowledgement, incident timelines, and automated status page updates when monitors change state. UptimeRobot's incident handling is more basic: it records incidents, but the management workflow is simpler.

Check Intervals

KeepUp offers 30-second check intervals on paid plans compared to UptimeRobot's 1-minute minimum. For services where every second of downtime matters, this is meaningful. On free plans, KeepUp checks every 3 minutes versus UptimeRobot's 5 minutes.

Pricing Comparison

Plan Level KeepUp UptimeRobot
Free5 monitors, 3-min checks, status page, 9 alert channels50 monitors, 5-min checks, basic status page, email alerts
Pro/Starter$9/mo — 20 monitors, 1-min checks, custom domain$7/mo — 50 monitors, 1-min checks, SMS alerts
Business/Pro$29/mo — 100 monitors, 30s checks, escalation policies$28/mo — 100 monitors, advanced features
EnterpriseCustom pricing$54/mo — advanced features

At the pro level, pricing is comparable. The difference is what you get for that price. KeepUp includes escalation policies, ISP integrations, and multi-language status pages. UptimeRobot includes more monitors on the free tier and a wider range of basic alert channels.

Who Should Choose UptimeRobot?

  • You need to monitor many endpoints for free (the 50-monitor free tier is unbeatable)
  • You need simple, no-frills monitoring without escalation complexity
  • You are already integrated into the UptimeRobot ecosystem with scripts and automations
  • You do not need ISP-specific features or multi-language status pages

Who Should Choose KeepUp?

  • You run an ISP and need IXC, Zabbix, or PPPoE monitoring
  • You need escalation policies with multi-step notification chains
  • You want a branded, multi-language status page that updates automatically
  • You need incident acknowledgement and management workflows
  • You serve customers in Brazil and need LGPD compliance and Portuguese support
  • You want monitoring, alerting, and status pages in one platform

The Verdict

Both tools are solid choices, but they serve different needs. UptimeRobot excels at simple, affordable, high-volume monitoring. KeepUp excels at integrated monitoring with professional status pages and advanced alerting. For ISPs and teams that need more than basic ping monitoring, KeepUp delivers more value at a comparable price point.

The best way to decide? Try both. UptimeRobot's free plan gives you 50 monitors. KeepUp's free plan gives you 5 monitors with all features unlocked. Spend a week with each and see which fits your workflow better.

title: Uptime Monitoring for Brazilian ISPs: A Complete Guide
excerpt: Everything Brazilian ISPs need to know about uptime monitoring: ANATEL requirements, IXC and Zabbix integration, PPPoE monitoring, LGPD compliance, and the tools built for the BR market.
content:
Brazil is home to over 20,000 registered Internet Service Providers, making it one of the most dynamic and fragmented ISP markets in the world. From small wireless operations in the Amazon to large FTTH providers in the Southeast, these companies face unique monitoring challenges that most global tools simply do not address. This guide covers everything you need to know about uptime monitoring for Brazilian ISPs.

The Brazilian ISP Landscape

Unlike the US or European markets, which are dominated by a handful of large telcos, Brazil's internet market has a vibrant ecosystem of regional ISPs. These providers, often members of ABRINT (Associacao Brasileira de Provedores de Internet e Telecomunicacoes), serve millions of subscribers in areas where major telcos like Claro, Vivo, and Oi have limited reach.

Most Brazilian ISPs share common characteristics:

  • FTTH infrastructure — fiber to the home is the standard for new deployments, typically using GPON technology with Huawei, ZTE, or Intelbras OLTs
  • IXC Soft for billing and provisioning — the dominant ERP system for Brazilian ISPs, managing subscriber accounts, billing, and network provisioning
  • Zabbix for network monitoring — the go-to open-source monitoring platform for network infrastructure
  • PPPoE authentication — the standard method for subscriber authentication on most Brazilian ISP networks
  • Regional focus — serving specific cities, neighborhoods, or rural areas with deep local knowledge

ANATEL Requirements for ISPs

ANATEL (Agencia Nacional de Telecomunicacoes) is Brazil's telecommunications regulatory agency. ISPs operating under SCM (Servico de Comunicacao Multimidia) licenses must comply with several requirements related to service quality and transparency:

Service Quality Indicators

ANATEL monitors several quality indicators that ISPs must track and report:

  • Disponibilidade — service availability percentage (target: 99% or higher)
  • Latencia — network latency to reference points
  • Velocidade — actual speed as a percentage of contracted speed
  • Perda de pacotes — packet loss rates

A proper uptime monitoring system helps ISPs track these indicators continuously, providing the data needed for regulatory reporting and compliance. KeepUp's monitoring dashboard can generate reports aligned with ANATEL's quality indicators.

Transparency Obligations

ISPs must communicate service disruptions to their subscribers. While ANATEL does not mandate a specific format, having a public status page provides clear, timestamped evidence of your communication efforts during incidents. This can be invaluable during audits or customer complaints filed with ANATEL's consumer platform.

Integrating with IXC Soft

IXC Soft is the backbone of most Brazilian ISP operations. It handles subscriber management, billing, provisioning, and equipment tracking. For uptime monitoring to be truly useful for a Brazilian ISP, it needs to integrate with IXC.

KeepUp offers native IXC integration that enables:

  • Service monitoring — check if IXC services are responding correctly and returning expected data
  • Equipment monitoring — monitor ONU/ONT status directly from IXC's equipment database, tracking signal levels and connectivity
  • Subscriber impact assessment — when network equipment goes down, IXC integration helps you understand how many subscribers are affected
  • Automated provisioning checks — verify that the provisioning system is correctly configuring new subscribers and service changes

The integration uses IXC's REST API with token-based authentication. Configuration requires your IXC base URL and API credentials, and KeepUp handles the rest.

Leveraging Existing Zabbix Infrastructure

Most Brazilian ISPs already run Zabbix for network monitoring. It monitors switches, routers, OLTs, servers, and other infrastructure components. Rather than duplicating this monitoring in a separate tool, the smart approach is to integrate with it.

KeepUp's Zabbix integration connects via Zabbix's JSON-RPC API to:

  • Import host availability — check if Zabbix hosts (network devices) are reachable
  • Monitor trigger states — pull trigger data from Zabbix to reflect network problems on your status page
  • Consolidate dashboards — see both application-level and network-level monitoring in one place

This means you do not have to choose between Zabbix and KeepUp. Use Zabbix for deep network monitoring and KeepUp for the customer-facing status page, alerting, and incident management layer on top.

PPPoE Monitoring Strategies

PPPoE (Point-to-Point Protocol over Ethernet) is the dominant authentication protocol for Brazilian ISP subscribers. Monitoring PPPoE health is critical because authentication failures directly impact customer connectivity.

Key metrics to monitor include:

  • RADIUS server availability — if your RADIUS server goes down, no new PPPoE sessions can be established
  • Active session count — a sudden drop in active PPPoE sessions often indicates a widespread connectivity issue
  • Authentication success rate — an increase in authentication failures may indicate configuration problems or equipment issues
  • BNG/concentrator health — the broadband network gateways that terminate PPPoE sessions need continuous monitoring

KeepUp can monitor RADIUS servers via port checks and API monitors, while Zabbix integration pulls PPPoE session data from your concentrators for comprehensive visibility.

LGPD Compliance Considerations

Brazil's Lei Geral de Protecao de Dados (LGPD) applies to any system that processes personal data, including monitoring tools. When choosing a monitoring platform, ISPs should consider:

  • Data residency — where monitoring data is stored and whether it can be kept within Brazil
  • Subscriber data handling — if your status page collects email addresses for notifications, that is personal data under LGPD
  • Data retention policies — monitoring logs should have configurable retention periods
  • Consent mechanisms — subscribers should explicitly opt in to email or SMS notifications
  • Data processing agreements — your monitoring vendor should have a DPA that covers LGPD requirements

KeepUp is designed with LGPD compliance in mind, including opt-in subscriber management, configurable data retention, and a clear privacy policy.

Building Your Monitoring Stack

For a Brazilian ISP, we recommend this monitoring architecture:

  1. Network layer (Zabbix) — monitor switches, routers, OLTs, servers, and bandwidth utilization
  2. Service layer (KeepUp) — monitor customer-facing services: DNS, RADIUS, customer portal, billing system
  3. Integration layer (KeepUp + Zabbix + IXC) — pull data from Zabbix and IXC into KeepUp for a unified view
  4. Communication layer (KeepUp status page) — present service status to customers with a branded, Portuguese-language status page
  5. Alert layer (KeepUp alerting) — notify your team via multiple channels with escalation policies

This architecture leverages your existing Zabbix investment while adding the customer communication and incident management layers that Zabbix alone cannot provide.

Getting Started

If you run a Brazilian ISP and want to improve your monitoring and customer communication, start with KeepUp's free plan. Set up a few monitors for your critical services, create a status page in Portuguese, and see the difference it makes when your next outage happens. Your customers will thank you, and your support team will too.

title: Status Page Best Practices: 12 Rules for Building Trust During Outages
excerpt: 12 battle-tested best practices for running a status page that builds customer trust during outages. From transparency rules to incident templates and subscriber management.
content:
A status page is only as good as the practices behind it. You can have the most beautiful, technically sophisticated status page in the world, but if your team does not update it promptly, communicate honestly, or manage incidents consistently, it will do more harm than good. Here are 12 rules we have learned from working with hundreds of teams that run status pages effectively.

Rule 1: Update Within 5 Minutes of Detection

The single most important practice is speed. When your monitoring system detects an issue, your status page should reflect it within 5 minutes. Not 15. Not "when we know more." Five minutes. Your customers are already experiencing the problem. They are checking your status page right now. If it says "All Systems Operational" while their service is down, you have lost credibility.

With KeepUp's automated status pages, monitor status changes update the page instantly, removing human delay from the equation.

Rule 2: Communicate What You Know, Not What You Don't

Your first status update does not need to explain the root cause. It needs to acknowledge the problem. "We are aware of issues affecting [service] and are investigating" is a perfectly good first update. You can add details as you learn them. Waiting until you have a full explanation before posting anything is one of the most common mistakes teams make.

Rule 3: Use Plain Language

Your subscribers are not network engineers. "We are experiencing elevated error rates on our primary load balancer cluster" should become "Some users may experience difficulty accessing our service." Save the technical details for your internal post-mortem. Your status page audience wants to know three things: what is broken, when it might be fixed, and what they should do in the meantime.

Rule 4: Post Regular Updates During Incidents

Silence during an incident is worse than no status page at all. Once you have acknowledged an issue, post updates every 15-30 minutes, even if the update is "We are still investigating and will provide another update in 30 minutes." This tells your customers that someone is actively working on the problem and they have not been forgotten.

Rule 5: Define Your Service Components Thoughtfully

Too few components and your status page is vague. Too many and it is overwhelming. Aim for 5-12 service components that map to what your customers actually use. Each component should represent something a customer would recognize. "Web Application," "API," "Email Delivery," and "Payment Processing" are good. "Database Primary," "Redis Cluster," and "Worker Queue" are not.

Rule 6: Use Severity Levels Consistently

Most status pages use a severity scale: Operational, Degraded Performance, Partial Outage, Major Outage. Define what each level means for your services and apply them consistently. If "Degraded Performance" sometimes means 200ms slower and sometimes means 50% of requests failing, your customers cannot trust the signals.

At KeepUp, we recommend these definitions:

  • Operational — all systems functioning within normal parameters
  • Degraded Performance — the service is available but slower or less reliable than usual
  • Partial Outage — the service is unavailable for some users or some functionality is affected
  • Major Outage — the service is unavailable for most or all users

Rule 7: Enable Subscriber Notifications

Not everyone will check your status page proactively. Enable email and SMS subscriptions so customers can get notified when incidents occur. This is especially important for B2B services and ISPs, where downstream businesses depend on your uptime. KeepUp supports email and SMS subscriber notifications out of the box.

Rule 8: Schedule Maintenance Windows in Advance

Every planned maintenance should be announced on your status page at least 48 hours in advance, preferably a week. Include the expected start time, end time, and which services will be affected. Surprises erode trust. Even if the maintenance takes less time than expected, post the window early and close it when you are done.

Rule 9: Write Honest Post-Incident Reviews

After every significant incident, publish a post-incident review (also called a postmortem). Be honest about what happened, why it happened, and what you are doing to prevent it from happening again. Customers respect transparency far more than perfection. The companies that earn the most trust are not the ones that never have outages. They are the ones that handle outages with honesty and accountability.

Rule 10: Monitor Your Status Page Itself

This sounds obvious, but we have seen it happen more times than you would expect: the status page goes down during an outage. Your status page should be hosted on infrastructure independent of the services it monitors. If your main application is down, your status page must remain accessible. KeepUp's status pages are hosted on independent infrastructure specifically for this reason.

Rule 11: Use Maintenance Banners for Non-Emergency Information

Status pages are not just for outages. Use maintenance banners or informational notices for things like upcoming changes, known issues that do not affect availability, or general announcements. This keeps your customers in the loop and makes your status page a destination for service information, not just crisis communication.

Rule 12: Review and Audit Quarterly

Every quarter, review your status page performance:

  • How quickly did you post initial updates during incidents?
  • Were update intervals consistent?
  • Did your severity levels accurately reflect the impact?
  • How many subscribers do you have? Is that number growing?
  • Are your service components still accurate, or has your architecture changed?
  • Did you publish post-incident reviews for all significant incidents?

This review does not need to be elaborate. A 30-minute meeting with your ops team once a quarter is enough to identify areas for improvement and keep your status page practices sharp.

Putting It All Together

These 12 rules boil down to a simple philosophy: be fast, be honest, and be consistent. Your customers do not expect perfection. They expect transparency. A status page that is updated quickly, written clearly, and maintained consistently will build more trust than any marketing campaign.

If you are looking for a platform that makes following these practices easier, try KeepUp for free. Automated status updates, subscriber notifications, maintenance windows, and incident management are all built in. You focus on fixing the problem. We will make sure your customers know what is happening.

title: How KeepUp's Alert System Works: Channels, Policies, and Escalation
excerpt: A deep dive into KeepUp's 3-layer notification architecture. Learn how alert channels, notification policies, and escalation chains work together to make sure the right person gets the right alert at the right time.
content:
Alerting is the bridge between detection and action. Your monitoring system can detect every outage perfectly, but if the right person does not find out in time, detection alone is worthless. That is why we built KeepUp's alerting system with three distinct layers: channels, policies, and monitor assignment. Each layer has a specific job, and together they create a flexible, reliable notification architecture.

The Three Layers of KeepUp Alerting

Most monitoring tools treat alerting as a simple pipeline: monitor detects problem, tool sends alert. KeepUp takes a more structured approach with three layers that give you granular control over who gets notified, how, and when.

Layer 1: Alert Channels

An alert channel is a configured connection to a notification service. It answers the question "how do we send this alert?" KeepUp supports nine channel types:

  1. Email — the universal default. Every team member has an email address, and email alerts work for non-urgent notifications. KeepUp sends HTML emails with monitor name, status, timestamp, and a direct link to the incident.
  2. Slack — send alerts to specific Slack channels. Useful for team visibility. Configure with a webhook URL and optionally customize the message format.
  3. Discord — similar to Slack integration. Send alerts to Discord channels via webhook. Popular with smaller teams and gaming companies.
  4. Telegram — send alerts to Telegram chats or groups via the Telegram Bot API. Especially popular in Brazil and other markets where Telegram usage is high.
  5. PagerDuty — trigger PagerDuty incidents for critical alerts. Integrates with PagerDuty's on-call scheduling and escalation system.
  6. OpsGenie — similar to PagerDuty. Trigger OpsGenie alerts that integrate with their on-call management platform.
  7. Webhooks — send a JSON payload to any URL. This is the escape hatch for custom integrations. Connect to your internal tools, automation systems, or any service that accepts webhooks.
  8. SMS — text message alerts for critical issues. When your team is not at their desk, SMS cuts through the noise. Particularly effective for on-call engineers and NOC teams.
  9. Microsoft Teams — send alerts to Teams channels via incoming webhook. Essential for organizations that use Microsoft 365 as their collaboration platform.

Each channel is configured independently with its own credentials, endpoint, and settings. You can have multiple channels of the same type (for example, one Slack channel for the engineering team and another for the support team).

Layer 2: Notification Policies

A notification policy is a set of rules that determines which channels are used and in what order. It answers the question "who gets notified, and when do we escalate?" This is where KeepUp's alerting becomes genuinely powerful.

A notification policy consists of one or more steps. Each step has:

  • Channels — which alert channels to trigger at this step
  • Delay — how long to wait before executing this step (0 for the first step, configurable for subsequent steps)
  • Condition — optionally, only execute this step if the incident has not been acknowledged

Here is a real-world example of a notification policy for a critical service:

Step Delay Channels Condition
1ImmediatelySlack (engineering), Email (on-call)None
25 minutesSMS (on-call engineer), PagerDutyNot acknowledged
315 minutesSMS (engineering manager), Telegram (NOC group)Not acknowledged
430 minutesSMS (VP of Engineering), Email (all-engineering)Not acknowledged

In this policy, the on-call engineer gets a Slack message and email immediately. If they do not acknowledge the incident within 5 minutes, SMS and PagerDuty kick in. If 15 minutes pass without acknowledgement, the engineering manager and NOC team are notified. At 30 minutes, it reaches the VP. This escalation chain ensures that critical incidents always reach someone who can act.

Layer 3: Monitor Assignment

The final layer connects monitors to notification policies. Each monitor can be assigned one or more notification policies, and you can assign different policies based on the monitor's importance. A critical production database might use the aggressive escalation policy above, while a development server might only use the first-step Slack notification.

This three-layer architecture means you can create reusable notification policies (for example, "Critical Escalation," "Standard Alert," "Informational Only") and assign them to monitors as needed. When your team structure changes, you update the policy once and it applies to all monitors using that policy.

Cooldown Periods

Nobody wants to receive 47 alerts for the same incident. KeepUp includes configurable cooldown periods that prevent duplicate notifications within a specified timeframe. If a monitor goes down and triggers an alert, the cooldown period ensures the same channel will not fire again for that monitor until the cooldown expires.

Cooldowns are configured per notification policy and apply per channel. You might set a 10-minute cooldown on Slack (so the channel does not get spammed) and a 30-minute cooldown on SMS (so you are not sending texts every few minutes). The escalation chain still works because escalation steps are separate events from repeated alerts.

Incident Acknowledgement

The acknowledgement system is tightly integrated with alerting. When an on-call engineer acknowledges an incident, two things happen:

  1. The escalation chain stops — subsequent steps in the notification policy will not fire (because the "not acknowledged" condition is no longer true)
  2. Other team members are notified — a brief message lets everyone know that someone is handling the incident

Engineers can acknowledge incidents via the web dashboard, by clicking a link in the email alert, or through the API. The goal is to make acknowledgement as frictionless as possible so engineers do it immediately rather than mentally noting it and forgetting to click the button.

Trigger Types

Not every alert is about downtime. KeepUp supports multiple trigger types:

  • Down — the monitor has detected that the service is unreachable or returning errors
  • Up (Recovery) — the service has recovered. Recovery alerts are important so your team knows when to stand down
  • Degraded — the service is responding but slower or less reliably than expected
  • SSL expiry warning — the SSL certificate is approaching expiration

You can configure notification policies to only trigger on specific types. Your Slack channel might get all alert types, while SMS only fires for down events. This prevents alert fatigue by routing the right severity to the right channel.

Alert Logs and Auditing

Every alert KeepUp sends is logged with full details: timestamp, channel, recipients, status (sent, failed, throttled), and any error messages. This audit trail is valuable for:

  • Debugging alert delivery issues ("Did the SMS actually send?")
  • Post-incident reviews ("Who was notified and when?")
  • Compliance requirements ("Can we prove we notified the team within our SLA?")
  • Optimizing your policies ("Are we sending too many alerts? Not enough?")

Setting Up Your First Policy

Getting started with KeepUp's alerting is straightforward:

  1. Create your channels — configure the notification services you use (email, Slack, SMS, etc.)
  2. Build a notification policy — start with a simple 2-step policy: immediate email + 5-minute SMS escalation
  3. Assign to monitors — attach the policy to your most critical monitors
  4. Test it — use KeepUp's test alert feature to verify delivery before a real incident

You can always add complexity later. Most teams start with a single policy and refine it over the first few weeks as they learn what works for their workflow.

Ready to set up alerting that actually works? Start free with KeepUp and configure your first notification policy in under 5 minutes. All nine alert channels are available on every plan.

title: Why Your ISP Needs a Public Status Page (And How to Set One Up)
excerpt: Your ISP is losing money every time a customer calls about an outage. A public status page can cut support tickets by 40% and build lasting trust. Here is the ROI math and how to get started.
content:
It is 7:42 PM on a Friday. Your ISP's core router just went down, and 3,000 subscribers lost their internet connection. Within minutes, your support line has a 45-minute hold time. Your WhatsApp group is chaos. Your team is spending more time answering "Is the internet down?" than actually fixing the problem.

This scenario plays out at ISPs every single day. And the solution is deceptively simple: a public status page.

The Hidden Cost of Not Having a Status Page

Let us do some math. Consider a mid-sized ISP with 10,000 subscribers.

Support Call Cost During an Outage

During a major outage, a typical ISP receives calls from 5-10% of affected subscribers. For a 10,000-subscriber ISP, that is 500 to 1,000 calls. Each call takes an average of 3 minutes to handle (including hold time, the "Is the internet down?" conversation, and post-call logging).

Metric Without Status Page With Status Page
Calls during major outage750300 (-60%)
Average call duration3 minutes2 minutes
Total support time37.5 hours10 hours
Cost at $15/hr per agent$562$150
Savings per incident—$412

If your ISP experiences just two major outages per month (and most experience more), a status page saves you roughly $800/month in direct support costs alone. That does not account for the improved customer experience, reduced churn, or the engineering time recaptured from not answering support calls.

Customer Churn Impact

Research consistently shows that customers do not leave because of outages. They leave because of poor communication during outages. A customer who knows what is happening and when to expect resolution is far more likely to stay than one who calls support, sits on hold for 30 minutes, and gets a vague answer.

If a public status page reduces your monthly churn by even 0.5%, the financial impact is significant. For a 10,000-subscriber ISP charging $30/month, a 0.5% churn reduction means retaining 50 additional subscribers per month, worth $1,500 in monthly recurring revenue.

What a Good ISP Status Page Looks Like

An effective ISP status page is not just a green-or-red dashboard. It is a communication tool that serves multiple audiences: subscribers checking if there is an outage, technical staff looking for details, and potential customers evaluating your transparency.

Essential Components

  • Service components by region — ISPs should break down their status by geographic region or service area. "Internet - North Zone" and "Internet - South Zone" are more useful than a single "Internet" component.
  • Current status with timestamp — show when the status was last updated. A status that says "Operational" with a "last updated 3 hours ago" timestamp is less trustworthy than one updated 2 minutes ago.
  • Active incidents with updates — every active incident should have a timeline of updates showing what is happening and what your team is doing.
  • Scheduled maintenance calendar — show upcoming maintenance windows so subscribers can plan around them.
  • Uptime history — a 90-day uptime chart shows your track record at a glance. If your uptime is good, this builds trust. If it is not great, the transparency still builds more trust than hiding the data.
  • Subscriber notifications — let subscribers sign up for email or SMS alerts. Proactive notification is vastly better than reactive checking.

Branding and Language

Your status page should look like it belongs to your company. Use your logo, your colors, and your brand voice. If your subscribers speak Portuguese, your status page should be in Portuguese. KeepUp supports multi-language status pages with custom branding and custom domains, so status.yourisp.com looks and feels like your brand.

Common Objections (And Why They're Wrong)

"We don't want to advertise our outages"

Your customers already know when you are down. They experience it firsthand. A status page does not create awareness of outages; it channels existing frustration into a constructive experience. The alternative is that customers vent their frustration on social media, review sites, or to your support team. A status page gives them the information they want, on your terms.

"Our competitors will use it against us"

Every ISP has outages. Your competitors know this. What they cannot compete against is transparency. A public status page signals confidence and professionalism. It says "we are reliable enough to be transparent about our performance." Ironically, the ISPs that publish their uptime data tend to be perceived as more reliable, not less.

"We don't have time to maintain it"

With automated monitoring and status pages, there is almost nothing to maintain. KeepUp automatically updates your status page when monitors detect issues. Your team only needs to add context updates during incidents, and with pre-written templates, even that takes under a minute.

"It's too expensive"

We showed the math above. A status page that reduces support calls by 60% during outages saves hundreds of dollars per incident. KeepUp's free plan includes a status page with 5 monitors. Even the paid plans cost less than a single hour of support agent time per month.

How to Set One Up in 15 Minutes

Setting up a status page for your ISP does not require a development team or a weekend project. With the right tool, you can be live in 15 minutes:

  1. Sign up — Create a free KeepUp account (no credit card required)
  2. Add your monitors — Set up HTTP, ping, or port monitors for your critical services (DNS, RADIUS, customer portal, etc.)
  3. Configure your status page — Add your logo, choose your colors, select the monitors to display, and set your preferred language
  4. Set up a custom domain — Point status.yourisp.com to KeepUp with a CNAME record
  5. Enable notifications — Turn on subscriber email and SMS notifications
  6. Share the link — Add the status page URL to your customer portal, support page, and email signatures

That is it. When a monitor detects an issue, your status page updates automatically. Subscribers get notified. Your support team can direct callers to the status page instead of explaining the situation 500 times.

The Trust Dividend

The real value of a status page is not in reduced support costs or lower churn, though both are real. The real value is trust. Trust is the most valuable asset an ISP can have. In a market where switching costs are low and competitors are a Google search away, the ISPs that earn and keep customer trust are the ones that survive and grow.

A public status page is one of the simplest, most cost-effective ways to build that trust. It tells your customers: we see you, we respect your time, and we are working on it.

Your subscribers deserve it. Your support team needs it. And your business will benefit from it.

Start free with KeepUp and give your ISP the status page it deserves.

title: Why Transparency Matters: How a Status Page Builds Customer Trust
content:
The Silence That Costs You Customers

Picture this: it's 2 PM on a Tuesday. Your network goes down. Within minutes, your support phone lines are ringing off the hook. Your inbox floods with angry emails. Twitter lights up with complaints. And the worst part? Your customers don't know if you're even aware of the problem.

This scenario plays out every single day at ISPs, SaaS companies, and hosting providers around the world. And every single time, the damage isn't just from the outage itself — it's from the silence that surrounds it.

We've seen it happen hundreds of times: a 30-minute outage that could have been a minor inconvenience becomes a full-blown PR crisis simply because customers were left in the dark. The outage ends, but the trust damage lingers for months.

The Real Cost of Keeping Customers in the Dark

When something breaks and you say nothing, here's what actually happens behind the scenes:

  • Support ticket floods: Every affected customer individually contacts support. A single outage affecting 5,000 customers can generate 500+ tickets in under an hour. Each ticket costs you time, money, and agent morale.
  • Social media amplification: Frustrated customers take to Twitter, Facebook, and Reddit. One angry post gets shared, and suddenly your outage is public knowledge — but on their terms, not yours. The narrative is out of your control.
  • Customer churn: Studies show that 32% of customers will stop doing business with a brand after just one bad experience. It's not the outage that drives them away — it's the feeling of being ignored.
  • Internal chaos: Your support team is overwhelmed answering the same question hundreds of times: "Is there a problem?" Meanwhile, your engineering team is distracted from actually fixing the issue because they're fielding status requests from managers.
  • Revenue loss: For B2B services, prolonged uncertainty during an outage can trigger SLA penalties, contract reviews, and competitive evaluations. The longer customers don't know what's happening, the more they question whether they chose the right provider.

The math is brutal. If your average support ticket costs $15 to handle and an outage generates 500 unnecessary tickets, that's $7,500 in support costs alone — on top of whatever the outage itself costs you. And that doesn't account for the customers who simply leave without saying a word.

How a Status Page Changes Everything

A public status page fundamentally shifts the dynamic from reactive to proactive communication. Instead of waiting for customers to come to you with questions, you're already answering them before they even pick up the phone.

Here's what happens when you have a well-maintained status page:

  1. Customers self-serve information. Instead of 500 customers calling support, they check your status page. They see: "We're aware of connectivity issues in the downtown area. Our team is actively working on it. ETA: 45 minutes." Problem acknowledged. Expectations set. Phone stays on the hook.
  2. Your narrative, your terms. You control how the outage is communicated. You provide context, timelines, and updates. There's no room for speculation or exaggerated social media posts when official information is readily available.
  3. Support teams can focus. With a status page absorbing the majority of "is there a problem?" inquiries, your support agents can focus on customers who have unique issues that actually need human attention.
  4. Engineering teams stay focused. When everyone — from the CEO to the newest support agent — can check the status page for updates, your engineers aren't constantly interrupted with status requests. They can focus on what matters: fixing the problem.
  5. Post-incident trust actually increases. Here's the counterintuitive part: companies that communicate transparently during outages often see customer trust increase after the incident. Customers think, "They were honest with me. They kept me informed. I trust them more now."

A Real-World Example

Consider this scenario: an ISP with 10,000 subscribers experiences a fiber cut that affects 2,000 customers. Without a status page, roughly 400 of those customers call support within the first 30 minutes. Hold times spike to 25 minutes. Customers hang up frustrated and post on social media. The ISP spends the next week doing damage control.

Now imagine the same ISP with a KeepUp status page. Within 3 minutes of detecting the issue, an incident is posted: "Fiber cut on Main Street corridor — technicians dispatched. Estimated restoration: 2 hours." Updates follow every 20 minutes. Subscribers who opted into notifications get SMS and email alerts automatically. Support calls? Down to 40. Social media complaints? Nearly zero. Customer satisfaction after the incident? Higher than before, because customers felt informed and respected.

The Psychology Behind Transparency

There's a well-documented psychological principle at work here: showing vulnerability builds credibility. When a company admits "yes, we have a problem, and here's exactly what we're doing about it," it signals confidence and competence — not weakness.

Think about the brands you trust most. Chances are, they're not the ones that pretend to be perfect. They're the ones that, when things go wrong, step up and communicate openly. They treat you like an adult who can handle the truth.

A status page is the embodiment of this principle. It says: "We respect you enough to tell you the truth about our service. We're not hiding behind silence or corporate speak. Here's what's happening, and here's what we're doing about it."

This level of transparency creates what psychologists call a trust reservoir — accumulated goodwill that protects you when things go wrong. Customers who have seen you communicate honestly during past incidents give you the benefit of the doubt during future ones.

How KeepUp Makes Transparency Effortless

We built KeepUp because we believe every service provider deserves a professional, reliable way to communicate with their customers during incidents. Here's what makes it different:

  • Customizable status pages: Your status page looks like your brand. Custom domains, logos, colors, and layouts. Your customers see a professional page that builds confidence, not a generic third-party tool. Learn more about our status page features.
  • Subscriber notifications: Customers subscribe to updates via email or SMS. When you post an incident update, they're notified instantly — no manual effort required. See our alerting capabilities.
  • Incident timeline: Every incident gets a clear, chronological timeline showing when it was detected, what actions were taken, and when it was resolved. This creates a permanent record of your transparency.
  • Internationalization (i18n): Serve customers in their language. KeepUp supports multiple languages out of the box, so your Brazilian customers see updates in Portuguese while your US customers see English.
  • Monitoring integration: KeepUp doesn't just display status — it actively monitors your infrastructure. When something goes down, we detect it automatically. No manual updates needed to get started. Perfect for ISPs of all sizes.
  • API-first architecture: Integrate KeepUp into your existing workflows. Update incidents from your ticketing system, chatbot, or CLI. Automate everything.

Best Practices for Running a Trustworthy Status Page

Having a status page is a great start, but how you use it matters just as much. Here are the practices that separate good status pages from great ones:

1. Update Frequently

During an active incident, post updates every 15-30 minutes — even if the update is "Our team is still investigating. No new information yet." Silence between updates creates anxiety. Regular updates, even without new information, signal that you're actively working on the problem.

2. Be Honest About Severity

Don't downplay issues. If your service is completely down, don't label it "degraded performance." Customers can tell when you're minimizing the problem, and it erodes trust faster than the outage itself. Use clear severity levels: Operational, Degraded, Partial Outage, Major Outage.

3. Provide ETAs (Even Rough Ones)

Customers don't need exact times — they need some sense of when things will be back to normal. "We expect to have this resolved within 2 hours" is infinitely more useful than silence. If you miss your ETA, update it and explain why. That's still better than no ETA at all.

4. Post a Retrospective

After a major incident, publish a post-mortem on your status page. Explain what happened, why it happened, and what you're doing to prevent it from happening again. This is the ultimate trust-building move.

5. Celebrate Uptime Too

Don't let your status page only be associated with bad news. Use it to showcase your uptime metrics, completed maintenance windows, and infrastructure improvements. Make it a destination customers visit to feel good about choosing you.

Start Building Trust Today

Every minute you operate without a public status page is a minute you're choosing reactive communication over proactive transparency. Your customers deserve better. Your support team deserves better. Your brand deserves better.

KeepUp gives you everything you need to communicate with confidence during outages — and to build the kind of trust that turns customers into advocates.

Create your free status page in 2 minutes and start showing your customers that transparency isn't just a buzzword for your company — it's a commitment.

title: How to Integrate KeepUp's API with Your Support System for Automatic Outage Detection
content:
The Problem: Your Support Team Is Flying Blind

It's 3 PM and your support queue is filling up. A customer opens a ticket: "My internet has been slow for the last 20 minutes." Your support agent checks the customer's account — everything looks normal. They start the usual troubleshooting: "Have you tried restarting your router?" Meanwhile, three more tickets come in with the same complaint. Then five more. Then twenty.

By the time someone on the support team realizes there's an actual network outage happening, 45 minutes have passed. Forty customers have been through unnecessary troubleshooting steps. Ten are furious. Three have already tweeted about it. And your engineering team? They've known about the issue for 30 minutes but nobody connected the dots between the support tickets and the network event.

This disconnect between your monitoring systems and your support workflows is one of the most expensive inefficiencies in service operations. Your monitoring tools know there's a problem. Your support team doesn't. And your customers are caught in the middle.

The Solution: Connect KeepUp's API to Your Support Workflow

KeepUp's API gives your support systems real-time access to monitoring data, incident status, and service health information. By integrating this data into your existing support tools, you can automatically detect when customer complaints are related to known outages — and respond accordingly, without any manual intervention.

The result? Faster response times. Happier customers. And support agents who can focus on problems that actually need human attention. Let's explore three powerful use cases.

Use Case 1: Chatbot Auto-Checks Status on Every Ticket

The most impactful integration is also the simplest: have your chatbot or ticket intake system automatically check KeepUp's API whenever a customer opens a support request.

How It Works

When a customer initiates a support conversation — whether through live chat, a web form, or an automated bot — your system makes a quick API call to KeepUp before routing the ticket to a human agent.

// Step 1: Check current monitor status
GET /api/v2/monitors
Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY

// Response includes all monitors with their current status
{
  "data": [
    {
      "id": 42,
      "name": "Core Network - Downtown",
      "status": "down",
      "last_checked": "2026-04-02T15:02:00Z"
    },
    {
      "id": 43,
      "name": "Core Network - Westside",
      "status": "up",
      "last_checked": "2026-04-02T15:02:00Z"
    }
  ]
}

// Step 2: If any monitors are down, check for active incidents
GET /api/v2/checks?monitor_id=42
Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY

If the API returns an active incident that matches the customer's location or service, the chatbot can immediately respond:

"We're currently aware of a connectivity issue affecting the Downtown area. Our engineering team has been working on it since 2:45 PM and estimates restoration by 4:00 PM. You can follow real-time updates on our status page. Is there anything else I can help you with?"

No hold time. No unnecessary troubleshooting. No frustrated customer. The entire interaction takes seconds instead of the typical 15-20 minute support call.

Implementation Skeleton

// Pseudo-code for chatbot integration
async function handleNewTicket(ticket) {
  // 1. Get customer's service area from their account
  const customerArea = await getCustomerArea(ticket.customerId);
  
  // 2. Check KeepUp for active incidents in that area
  const monitors = await keepupAPI.get('/api/v2/monitors');
  const affectedMonitors = monitors.filter(
    m => m.area === customerArea && m.status !== 'up'
  );
  
  // 3. If there's a known issue, auto-respond
  if (affectedMonitors.length > 0) {
    const incident = await keepupAPI.get(
      `/api/v2/checks?monitor_id=${affectedMonitors[0].id}`
    );
    return autoRespond(ticket, incident);
  }
  
  // 4. Otherwise, route to human agent as normal
  return routeToAgent(ticket);
}

Use Case 2: Webhook-Driven Auto-Replies to Affected Customers

The second use case flips the model: instead of checking KeepUp when a ticket comes in, KeepUp notifies your helpdesk when an outage is detected — and your helpdesk automatically reaches out to potentially affected customers.

How It Works

KeepUp sends webhook notifications whenever a monitor changes status or an incident is created or updated. Your helpdesk system listens for these webhooks and takes automated action. See our alerting documentation for webhook configuration details.

// Webhook payload when an incident is created
POST https://your-helpdesk.com/webhooks/keepup
{
  "event": "incident.created",
  "data": {
    "incident_id": 789,
    "title": "Connectivity issues - Downtown area",
    "status": "investigating",
    "affected_monitors": [42, 43],
    "created_at": "2026-04-02T14:45:00Z"
  }
}

When your helpdesk receives this webhook, it can:

  1. Identify affected customers by cross-referencing the affected monitors/areas with your customer database.
  2. Auto-tag open tickets from those customers as "related to known outage" so agents don't waste time troubleshooting.
  3. Send proactive notifications to affected customers before they even contact support: "We've detected an issue in your area and are working to resolve it. No action needed on your end."
  4. Auto-close resolved tickets when KeepUp sends the "incident.resolved" webhook, with a polite message explaining the issue has been fixed.

Imagine the impact: a customer is about to pick up the phone to complain, and they receive a message from you acknowledging the issue before they even had to ask. That's the kind of proactive service that builds lasting loyalty.

Webhook Handler Example

// Pseudo-code for webhook handler
async function handleKeepUpWebhook(payload) {
  switch (payload.event) {
    case 'incident.created':
      // Find all customers in affected area
      const customers = await findAffectedCustomers(
        payload.data.affected_monitors
      );
      
      // Tag any open tickets from these customers
      await tagOpenTickets(customers, payload.data.incident_id);
      
      // Send proactive notification
      await sendBulkNotification(customers, {
        subject: 'Service Update',
        message: `We've detected ${payload.data.title}. ` +
          'Our team is investigating. We\'ll update you shortly.'
      });
      break;
      
    case 'incident.resolved':
      // Auto-close related tickets
      await closeRelatedTickets(payload.data.incident_id, {
        message: 'The issue has been resolved. ' +
          'Your service should be back to normal.'
      });
      break;
  }
}

Use Case 3: Live Status Dashboard Widget for Support Agents

The third use case is the simplest to implement and provides enormous value: embed a live KeepUp status widget directly in your support agent's dashboard.

How It Works

Your support dashboard makes periodic API calls to KeepUp and displays the current status of all monitors in a sidebar or header widget. When an agent picks up a call or opens a ticket, they can immediately see if there are any known issues — no need to check a separate tool or ask the NOC team.

// Poll KeepUp API every 30 seconds for dashboard widget
GET /api/v2/monitors
Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY

// Display logic
monitors.forEach(monitor => {
  if (monitor.status === 'down') {
    showAlert(`${monitor.name} is currently DOWN`);
  } else if (monitor.status === 'degraded') {
    showWarning(`${monitor.name} is experiencing issues`);
  }
});

This gives agents instant context. When a customer calls about slow internet, the agent glances at the widget and sees: "Core Network - Downtown: DOWN since 2:45 PM." Instead of spending 15 minutes troubleshooting, the agent immediately says: "I can see we're experiencing an issue in your area. Our team is already on it. Let me give you the latest update..."

The customer feels heard. The agent feels empowered. The call takes 2 minutes instead of 15. Everyone wins.

How This Reduces MTTR (Mean Time to Resolution)

Integrating KeepUp with your support system doesn't just improve the customer experience — it directly reduces your Mean Time to Resolution in several ways:

  • Faster detection: When support ticket volume spikes correlate with KeepUp alerts, your team gets confirmation faster that a widespread issue exists — not just isolated complaints.
  • Eliminated false troubleshooting: Agents stop wasting 15-20 minutes per ticket running through troubleshooting scripts for issues that are actually network-wide outages. That time adds up fast.
  • Better prioritization: When your support system knows which tickets are outage-related and which are genuine individual issues, agents can prioritize effectively. The one customer with a unique problem doesn't get lost in a flood of outage tickets.
  • Faster escalation: Automated correlation between support volume and monitoring alerts helps your team escalate to engineering faster, with better data about impact scope.
  • Reduced resolution time: When engineers aren't being interrupted by support requests for status updates (because the status page and integrations handle that automatically), they resolve issues faster. Period.

We've seen KeepUp users reduce their average MTTR by 40-60% after implementing API integrations with their support systems. That's not just an efficiency gain — it's a fundamental improvement in service quality. For ISPs, this translates directly to better SLA compliance and reduced churn.

The Dream Scenario

Picture this: a customer opens a support ticket at 3:05 PM. Your chatbot instantly checks KeepUp's API and responds within 3 seconds:

"Hi! We're currently aware of a connectivity issue affecting your area. Our engineering team has been working on it since 2:50 PM, and we estimate service will be fully restored by 3:30 PM. You can track real-time updates at status.yourcompany.com. We'll also send you a notification when everything is back to normal. Is there anything else I can help you with?"

The customer is informed. They feel respected. They know you're on it. They close the chat and go about their day. Total interaction time: 15 seconds.

Now multiply that by every customer affected by the outage. Instead of 200 support calls averaging 12 minutes each (that's 40 hours of agent time), you have 200 automated responses averaging 15 seconds each. Your support team is free. Your customers are happy. Your engineers are focused on fixing the problem.

That's the power of connecting KeepUp to your support workflow.

Getting Started Is Easy

You don't need to implement all three use cases at once. Start with the one that addresses your biggest pain point:

  1. If customers are frustrated by long hold times: Start with Use Case 1 (chatbot integration). It's the quickest win with the highest customer impact.
  2. If you want to be proactive: Start with Use Case 2 (webhook-driven notifications). It's the most impressive from a customer experience perspective.
  3. If your agents need better context: Start with Use Case 3 (dashboard widget). It's the simplest to implement and improves every single support interaction.

KeepUp's API is RESTful, well-documented, and designed for exactly these kinds of integrations. Whether you're connecting to Zendesk, Freshdesk, Intercom, a custom helpdesk, or even a simple Telegram bot, the patterns are the same.

Get your API key and start integrating today. Your support team — and your customers — will thank you.
